name: Basic Hand Tools (Hammer, Chisel, Saw)
tagline: Percussion, cutting, and ripping tools that convert human intent into shaped wood, stone, and metal
problem:
Finger pressure cannot split a log, mortise a joint, or cut plate steel. Humans need hardened working edges and mass delivery systems — hammers for impulse, chisels for controlled splitting along weak planes, saws for kerf removal through abrasion and chip clearance. Without this triad, carpentry, joinery, and rough metal forming remain painfully slow.

The design problem is not only sharpness but durability: edges chip, handles split, heat ruins temper. Each tool is a system: head mass, handle compliance, grip geometry, and maintenance ritual (peening handles, filing teeth, jointing saw plates).

These tools are also safety-critical: glancing blows, tooth kickback, and flying chips define workshop epidemiology. Technique is inseparable from hardware.
overview:
A hammer stores kinetic energy in a moving head and transfers it through a small contact patch. Hard faces elastically drive nails; soft faces drive chisels without marring steel. Mass and handle length set impact velocity trade space — heavy heads accelerate slowly but deliver momentum.

Chisels are wedges with controlled bevel angles: steep bevels for mortising tough wood; shallow bevels for paring across grain. Metal chisels add heat treatment — hard martensitic edge, softer tough back.

Saws are rows of cutting teeth with rake and set: set widens kerf so the plate does not bind; rake and gullet shape control chip formation in rip versus crosscut profiles. Sharpening is jointing (uniform height), shaping (filing angles), and setting (bending alternately).

Bronze and iron ages swap materials but not functions: the biomechanics of swing, stance, and sightlines remain. Machine tools later automate these motions, but hand tools remain repair instruments when power fails.

principles.name: Impulse and momentum
principles.explanation: Hammer blow efficacy depends on momentum at impact; elastic rebound wastes energy unless managed (e.g., dead-blow fillers).

principles.name: Wedge action
principles.explanation: Chisels and axes concentrate force along a line, driving cracks along grain or shear planes in metal swarf.

principles.name: Cutting geometry
principles.explanation: Positive rake slices; negative rake scrapes. Tooth geometry must match fibre orientation — rip teeth act like chisels; crosscut teeth shear fibres.

principles.name: Stress risers
principles.explanation: Square corners at hammer eyes or chisel sockets initiate cracks; rounding and gradual tapers improve fatigue life.

components.name: Hammer head
components.function: Delivers impact; may include pein or claw
components.madeFrom: Wrought iron, steel, stone maul prehistoric
components.criticalNote: Secure wedge pin — loose heads are projectiles

components.name: Handle (haft)
components.function: Provides lever arm and shock damping via flex
components.criticalNote: Grain runout at eye equals failure

components.name: Chisel blade
components.function: Cuts wood or metal by shearing and wedging
components.madeFrom: Hardened steel edge welded to softer body in quality tools
components.criticalNote: Do not use sideways pry loads — snaps tang

components.name: Saw plate and teeth
components.position: Tensioned in frame (frame saw) or handheld
components.criticalNote: Rust increases binding — oil lightly, not sticky

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Basic Hand Tools (Hammer, Chisel, Saw) is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Tool steel or shear steel
earthLocations: Historical shear steel from refined blister iron
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Imported stock until rolling mills exist
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Forge, harden, temper to straw/blue per role

rawMaterials.name: Handle wood
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Air dry to 8–12% moisture before fitting

rawMaterials.name: Wedge and pin stock
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Epoxy secondary — not primary structural early

buildSteps.title: Forge or grind hammer head blank
buildSteps.description: Upset stock to mass; punch eye hot; drift to final eye size parallel to striking face. Dress faces flat and perpendicular to eye axis.

buildSteps.title: Fit handle with interference and wedge
buildSteps.description: Burn handle in slightly oversize eye; trim shoulder seat; drive wooden wedge across grain; add steel wedge if needed.
buildSteps.warningNote: Loose handle after first day of use — re-wedge before next swing

buildSteps.title: Grind and heat-treat chisel
buildSteps.description: Normalize, harden edge zone, temper back for toughness. Flatten back; hone bevel at consistent angle.
buildSteps.warningNote: Overhard chisels chip on knot impacts

buildSteps.title: Shape and sharpen saw teeth
buildSteps.description: Joint teeth level; file rip or crosscut profiles; set teeth with saw set tool; test cut in scrap matching work species.
buildSteps.warningNote: Dull saws require excess force — primary kickback cause

buildSteps.title: Proof and maintain
buildSteps.description: Strike cold chisels only with appropriate hammer faces; peen hammer handle swell if loose; store saws dry and lightly oiled.
buildSteps.warningNote: Never use a claw hammer on hardened steel tools — face chips become shrapnel

history.event: Ground stone axes and adzes precede metal, establishing hafting and edge maintenance cultures.
Metal axes, chisels, and saws appear in carpentry contexts with preserved timber joinery.
Mass-produced crucible-steel chisels and spring-steel saws spread through building trades as industrial toolmaking standardises alloys and heat treatment.

impact: Hand tools are the debugger of civilisation: when power fails, roads crack, or factories stop, chisels and saws still modify reality. They are also pedagogical — teaching grain, hardness, and human factors before anyone touches a lathe.
